feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

paragraphs.text: On the basis of the facts which I shall set out in turn, based on my personal experience, in particular Slovakia, and the experience of individuals, which I am aware of, in EU countries, consumer protection in the field of digitalisation – wherever wireless communication and radio frequency electromagnetic radiation-emitting equipment is used – has been insufficient for a long time, and the policy of the European Commission and the Member States has allowed industry to engage in unfair commercial practices vis-à-vis ordinary consumers, resulting in health, safety and privacy risks. The industry, through its lobbyists and its advisors in the European institutions and in the Member States, promotes its interests to the detriment of consumers. The ordinary consumer has significantly fewer possibilities than to pursue his or her interests, which puts him at a fundamental disadvantage compared to industry.

(a) The credibility of ICNIRP, whose recommendations are guided by the European Commission, is fundamentally called into question, for example by statements published by insider Prof. Dariusz Leszczyński, which, inter alia, refers to a non-transparent selection of members, results in a common view among members who do not consider it necessary to examine the non-thermal effects of radio frequency electromagnetic radiation, which is remarkably fully suited to industry. There is also a lack of research on the effects of concurrent effects of multiple health and environmental burdens.
(source: www.instytutsprawobywatelskich.pl/smartfony-wi-fi-i-puszka-pandory-co-wynika-z-badan-Promieniowania-electomagnetycznego-elektrosmogu/
This also puts into question the credibility of the European Commission and the countries, including Slovakia, which follow the ICNIRP recommendations.

b) According to the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC), which is part of the World Health Organisation (WHO), radio-frequency electromagnetic fields are classified as 'a suspected (possible) carcinogen for humans'. This means that they are classified in group 2b. (source: www.iarc.who.int/wp-content/uploads/2018/07/pr208_E.pdf) This is a reason for strict application of the precautionary principle, which however does not apply in this area.
c) According to the Budapest Declaration, a commitment has also been made at international level to address and respond to the problem of electromagnetic radiation.

(source: iris.who.int/bitstream/handle/10665/371461/Budapest-decl-2023-eng.pdf?sequence=5) This is the reason for a thorough investigation of the effects of electromagnetic radiation on the environment, human, animal and plant health, which is however ignored.

In the following points, I will address the risks, abuses and ambiguities regarding remote readings of consumption meters and RDPs (heat cost allocators) in multi-apartment buildings. The relevant Slovak authorities have been informed of these issues and asked to remedy them, we have not received any promise to remedy them (source e.g.: https://www.elektrosmogazdravie.sk/wp-content/uploads/docs/zapisnica_MHSR_15_7_2025.pdf)

The issue of remote readings of consumption meters and RDPs (NB: this is a synonym for PRT – heat allocator) in multi-apartment buildings, particularly for the population of multi-apartment buildings, a significant proportion of the population of Slovakia and the European Union. These residents are consumers because, according to Resolution of the Supreme Court of the Slovak Republic, ref. 1Vcdo/5/2019 consumer protection rules also apply to management contracts pursuant to Section 8a of Act No 182/1993 on the ownership of apartments and non-residential premises concluded between a management company and owners of apartments and non-residential premises who are natural and legal persons.

Management companies often promote the narrative that those remote deductions are mandatory because this is mandated by the Energy Efficiency Directive 2012/27/EU (possibly analogous Directive 2023/1791/EU) (https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/SK/TXT/PDF/?URI=CELEX:02012L0027-20230504) and its transposition into Act No 657/2004 Coll. on thermal energy and Act No 321/2014 Coll. on energy efficiency.

In fact, Articles 9a, 9b and 9c of the Directive (and also those laws) do not entail an unconditional requirement for the remote reading of heat meters, domestic hot water meters and heat sub-meters, since, in order to be installed, the condition of technical feasibility and cost-

effectiveness in terms of proportionality with regard to possible energy savings must be met. This condition is crucial: it follows from the objectives of the Directive (in accordance with paragraph 2 of the preamble, in its first version, and also under Article 1, Article 3, as last consolidated version of 4.5.2023, the aim of the directive is to reduce energy consumption or to save energy consumed in the EU in any form whatsoever) for which the directive was adopted, as well as from the energy savings obligation in Article 7 of the Directive. By introducing more complex and expensive meters and RDPs with radio modules, the above-mentioned necessary condition is not fulfilled, since those meters and RDPs with radio modules themselves consume energy from batteries, which will become hazardous waste in several years, entailing additional costs for their disposal and treatment, requiring new technical equipment for receiving, transmitting, processing and storing data which, in addition, entails new risks (compared to meters and RDPNs without radio modules) – health, safety and privacy losses – and these risks entail additional handling and management costs, as well as the costs of preventing those risks.
Another argument put forward by the management companies is that that directive and the laws which introduced it impose an unconditional obligation to deduct remotely from 1.1.2027.
In fact, pursuant to point 2 of Article 9c of that directive,

'Measuring instruments and heat cost allocators which are not remotely readable but are already installed shall be equipped with a remote reading function or replaced with remotely readable devices by 1 January 2027, unless the Member State concerned demonstrates that this is not cost-effective.
However, this has been transposed differently by the Slovak legislators:

Pursuant to Section 17(4)(b) of Act No 657/2004 on thermal energy and Section 11(10) of Act No 321/2014 on energy efficiency, the requirement to be equipped with a remote reading function does not apply if the owner of the building proves that the installation of the remote reading function is not cost-effective or technically possible.

It follows from the above that the above-mentioned EU Directive is not correctly transposed into Slovak legislation. Moreover, the burden of proof from the State has been passed on by the Slovak legislators to the building owners who are consumers, which is contrary to consumer protection rules.

Under the above-mentioned EU Directive and Slovak law, the obligation to equip the remote reading function does not concern the measurement of cold water consumption. Managers ignore this and penalise the refusal of the remote reading of cold water meters.

The term 'remote deduction' is not defined in legislation. In practice, management companies shall only promote risk-based and inefficient remote readings by means of radio modules mounted on consumption meters and RDPs. A less risky approach could be to deduct via a data cable (applied in certain multi-apartment buildings abroad) or self-deduction and subsequent data entries by the users of the apartment themselves, for example via www.samoodpocet.sk, appear to be the most appropriate way of doing so.

There is an assessment on consumption meters and RDPs with radio modules supplied by Techem and commonly assembled in Slovakia, a summary of which we have been able to obtain. According to the above summary of the assessment, Techem's radio systems with regard to the scope of the Swiss limit values are not intended for sensitive sites. Sensitive sites are rooms in buildings that are regularly occupied or occupied by persons and playgrounds. Thus, in Switzerland, and not only there, they are aware that children are more vulnerable to radio-frequency electromagnetic fields. The same summary of the report also states that it is not possible to rule out the negative health impacts of those Techem radio systems on persons located in the premises where such meters are installed.

Radiofrequency electromagnetic fields are classified as 'asuspected (possible) carcinogen for humans' according to the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC), which is part of the World Health Organisation (WHO). This means that they are classified in Group 2B.
With regard to security risks and the risk of loss of privacy, it should be noted that the household in which such meters are installed, or RDPNs with radio modules, may be monitored on

the basis of the data they transmit, and these data may form the basis for identifying behavioural patterns applied in that household – e.g. when they occur in the morning, when they go to work, when they come from work, when they are showered, when they go to sleep, when they are travelling, etc.

Ultimately, such a household may be more vulnerable to looting, which may be reflected in higher insurance assets. More importantly, this way of monitoring household members can more easily become the target of criminal or other unfair activity, which may have an impact on the level of their life insurance premiums.

This may have an impact on reducing the quality of life of residents of such an apartment. At home, meters and RDPs with radio data are at greater risk than a mobile phone because it can be switched off, moved, can be evaded without any penalty. An even more malicious way of exploiting such meters can occur when an attacker is able to manipulate consumption data. As a last resort, the owner of the apartment may increase consumption data to the extent that it eventually deprives it of the apartment. Or someone can increase consumption data only slightly and another (but in some way with an attacker related) to the owner of an apartment in the same multi-apartment building may slightly reduce consumption data, which will not raise attention in the overall billing of consumption in the multi-apartment building. The ability to manipulate consumption data may also be misused when there is a need to prove someone's presence or absence in a given apartment over a certain period of time, making or destroying an alibi.

Another type of abuse may be the trading of (electronically) collected data on the consumption and customs of occupants of dwellings, which can then be used for targeted advertising, offering goods and services, or targeted propaganda or manipulation or for other purposes. The (electronic) collection, evaluation and marketing of large amounts of human data is currently a significant trend in information and communication technologies.

If in the past, for a person who refused to smoke cigarettes, to refuel leaded petrol who did not procure a risky product, there was currently no risk of penalties for the management companies. They penalise the refusal to use risky and more cost-intensive metering technology and RDPs with radio modules, and enforce the non-payment of the penalty by judicial means. In doing so, administrators reject the use of cheaper, risk-free technology. However, this has the effect that the owner of the apartment penalised is motivated not to investigate and is therefore contrary to the objectives of the EU Directive to be saved. In cases where management companies penalise such an owner of an apartment in the manner described above, the management contract provided for in the legislation in force is non-cancellable by the owner of the apartment. The owner who is damaged by the administrator with the consent of the other owners of the apartments in the multi-apartment building is a hostage.

New technologies will give us a better quality, fairer, safer and more privacy-friendly life, more energy efficient devices and processes. The result is very often the opposite. There are more and more people who complain about health problems, a lower sense of safety, higher insurance homes, preference for commercial interests over ordinary people's interests, over the protection of the environment.

All this is why the European Commission should immediately abolish the remote reading obligation in Directives 2012/27/EU and (EU) 2023/1791 in order to protect consumers from unnecessary new risks and manifest inefficiencies in remote readings. Consumers would be free to choose whether or not to install remote readings on their meters or RDPs in their homes. Self-deduction of specified meters, when consumers themselves report the counters of these meters, appears to be the most efficient means of reporting consumption. After a certain period of validity of the metrological verification (normally 5 years), the maintenance agent must, in any event, physically arrive at these meters for metrologically verified, the state of the meter being endorsed by the owner of the apartment by signing the meter, and must then reimburse the consumption thus established in the bill.
